title: Carnival Zombie 2nd Edition - Initial Thoughts on the agonizing choices!
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=j3BrzaBGJqY

Daniel (@dungeondive) returns with preliminary thoughts on **Carnival Zombie: Second Edition**, and leads with its defining quality: agonizing decisions. Multiple times a turn you face choices you'll genuinely stress over — a tense, nail-biting game he likens in feel (not form) to Space Hulk.

Six survivors flee a Venice built atop a waking leviathan, with three days to escape by boat, bridge, airship — or by lobbing a "holy bomb" down a church well into the monster's rotting heart. Days give you just four hours to move through flooding alleyways, build barricades, and search each character's *own* item deck; nights are a siege as undead pour in from every quadrant toward your refuge. Combat is dead simple (no dice — hits and wounds), so the complexity lives entirely in the punishing difficulty and the tyranny of too many good options.

His standout: the *corpse pile*, a little dexterity board where killed zombies are dropped, and any that tumble off come back to life — a tension-and-laughter generator he thinks will shine in a group. Gripes: fiddly boss iconography forcing constant reference-sheet checks. But it's fast to set up, plays superbly solo, and he heartily recommends it.

----

Carnival Zombie's difficulty comes from having too many good options and too little time, not from complex rules. Do you prefer that kind of agonizing-choice pressure, or a meatier rules-driven challenge?
